final_answer:
They shouldn’t; those are stale bypass-era tests, and that assumption is wrong.

Why it matters: they create invalid expectations (nil/disabled `GiltConsensus` paths) and waste time because they fail for the wrong reason instead of validating real Gilt execution behavior.

Why there is an `eth` test: Gilt execution is a geth fork, so execution-layer code lives under `bridge/gilt-exec/eth`; those tests are still Gilt-chain execution tests, not Ethereum-mainnet consensus tests.

What should be done next: remove/replace tests that depend on “no chain/no `GiltConsensus`” assumptions, and keep only deterministic unit tests plus integration tests that run with explicit Gilt execution + explicit `GiltConsensus` client wiring.
